critic_raw:
This design proposes a multi-modal deep learning model integrating EHR data and genomic sequencing for 30-day readmission prediction.

The split strategy contains an explicit self-identified flaw that the design then does not fix. Section 2 states 'Partitioning will use stratified random sampling across all available years' and acknowledges 'This approach aims to maintain class balance but may unintentionally mix future and past data in training and test sets.' This is precisely temporal leakage, and the design acknowledges it without correcting it. Readmission prediction models in clinical deployment are trained on past data and applied to future patients; a random split that mixes 2019 patients in the test set with 2020 patients in training is not representative of this deployment scenario.

Second, the test set protocol contains a critical violation. Section 6 states: 'After validation-based hyperparameter selection, a final check is run on the test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training on the combined train+validation set. This guards against validation-window overfitting and ensures the final model is not tuned to an unrepresentative period.' This is explicit test set peeking for model selection. If the model configuration is adjusted or a different configuration is selected based on test set performance, the test set is no longer a held-out evaluation — it becomes another validation set. The claimed protection against validation overfitting is exactly what invalidates the test set as an honest estimate of generalization.

Third, the multi-modal model includes clinical BERT embeddings from discharge summaries. Discharge summaries contain explicit mention of subsequent care plans, follow-up appointments, and sometimes direct references to readmission risk. If the discharge summary text encodes the treating clinician's implicit readmission risk assessment (e.g., 'patient is medically complex and may require readmission'), the transformer is not learning to predict readmission from patient state but is reading the clinician's assessment — which is itself predictive because clinicians have access to information not captured in structured EHR fields.

Fourth, genomic sequencing data is described as available for all patients in the cohort. Genomic sequencing availability at a healthcare system is highly selective — it is more common for cancer patients, younger patients, patients at academic medical centers, and patients enrolled in specific research protocols. The 'inclusion criterion' of all inpatients with complete EHR records and linked genomic sequencing creates a highly selected population that is systematically different from the general readmission population, and the design does not characterize this selection or assess whether the multi-modal advantage would generalize to the unsequenced majority.
all_issues_raised: The design explicitly acknowledges that stratified random sampling may mix future and past data in train and test sets but does not correct this; the acknowledged temporal leakage means the test set is not representative of the deployment scenario where training precedes evaluation in time.

Section 6 describes explicit test set peeking for model selection: if the selected configuration does not 'maintain its advantage' on the test set, adjustments are made; this invalidates the test set as an honest held-out estimate of generalization performance.

Discharge summary BERT embeddings likely encode clinician readmission risk assessments embedded in the narrative text, not just patient state; the multi-modal model may be predicting from clinician judgment rather than from patient biology, which is a form of label leakage through clinical narrative.

Genomic sequencing availability is highly selective (cancer patients, academic center enrollees, research protocol participants), so the cohort with complete genomic data is systematically different from the general readmission population; the multi-modal advantage reported on this selected cohort does not establish generalizability.
all_issues_adjudicated: The design explicitly acknowledges that stratified random sampling may mix future and past data in train and test sets but does not correct this; the acknowledged temporal leakage means the test set is not representative of the deployment scenario where training precedes evaluation in time.

Section 6 describes explicit test set peeking for model selection: if the selected configuration does not 'maintain its advantage' on the test set, adjustments are made; this invalidates the test set as an honest held-out estimate of generalization performance.

Discharge summary BERT embeddings likely encode clinician readmission risk assessments embedded in the narrative text, not just patient state; the multi-modal model may be predicting from clinician judgment rather than from patient biology, which is a form of label leakage through clinical narrative.

Genomic sequencing availability is highly selective (cancer patients, academic center enrollees, research protocol participants), so the cohort with complete genomic data is systematically different from the general readmission population; the multi-modal advantage reported on this selected cohort does not establish generalizability.
